# Transcrição: 04- Optimizing for Attribution & Conversion Windows 

- **Fonte:** 04- Optimizing for Attribution & Conversion Windows 
- **Duração:** 08:26
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Alright, guys, so I want to talk to you today about attribution windows. Now I'm in an ad account here, and the way that we can check out attribution windows is under our settings, so this changed a little bit. So I'm going to go under settings here. And if I scroll down, you'll see here it's got the attribution settings for this specific ad accounts. So attribution is essentially how far out Facebook will track results from your campaigns. What this means is that, let's say I click your Facebook ad today, and I check out your product page, and I really like what you have to sell, but I don't get paid till next week. So I decide to sit on it, and then next week when I get paid, I go back to that link and I purchase something. Well, the attribution window means that depending on how far out you have that, you'll be able to assign that conversion to your Facebook ads or not. So let's say that my attribution window was the default of 28-day clicks, which is what it is here. Essentially if I came back one week later and purchased that product, the conversion would be attributed to the last ad that I clicked or viewed inside the campaigns that you're running. So that's essentially what an attribution window is. Now this is a bit different to those, especially those of you that have been working with Google AdWords because Google AdWords has a one-time or a straight to purchase, straight to action attribution window. Whereas what you'll see here is Facebook has a 28-day attribution window for clicks, and you can also make this 28 days for view. However, the default is usually one day, and I would leave it as that. Now what this means is that if someone performs an action, such as purchasing something or becoming a lead or asking for a quote or anything like that, after 28 days or within 28 days of clicking your ad, that action, that conversion will be attributed back to the last ad that they clicked. This also means that if they perform the action within one day of seeing the ad, so maybe they didn't even click on it, but they saw it, it will be attributed to the last ad that they saw within a one-day attribution window. You can go into your ad account settings and essentially edit this or manage this. 28 days click and one day view is the default settings. We normally leave it as this, and 28 days is also the maximum. For those of you that have very high ticket products, such as maybe it's costing $3,000

## [03:02 - 06:06]

or $2,000 an ad, you may find that your purchase cycle, your customer purchase cycle, is a actually longer than 28 days. People need more time to get their funds together. This is where attribution windows can get a little bit tricky. It can be a little bit hard to track the effectiveness of your ads if you have very, very high ticket items for sale. That's where you want to start to get into offline event tracking. You'd set yourself up for offline event tracking and there's separate training on this in the membership site. Essentially that will then allow you to track back any purchases that occurred outside of this attribution window. Attribution window is for your reporting. What will show up inside of your reporting, and that's totally different to conversion windows. Attribution windows is more optimization and delivery. Attribution window is reporting. Conversion windows is optimization. What I'm meaning by conversion windows is this little thing down inside of the ad set. You're probably familiar with this and have seen this before. This is your conversion window. Essentially it sounds very, very similar, but the difference here is that what you're asking Facebook is to optimize your ad delivery based on actions taken within a specific time frame, so actions or a view of the ad. Let's say for example that you are selling a $3 product and it doesn't really need that much time or consideration. People should just be converting on the spot. Technically speaking, someone that then purchases within seven days is more of what Facebook would call a coincidental purchase. Most of your customers at that price point should be purchasing or taking an action within a one day time frame. By selecting the one day after click, essentially what you're telling Facebook is, look, I just want you to optimize for the people or optimize based on the data of the people that take that action within a one day click window, not seven days because seven days is more of coincidental, it's more accidental. We really don't want to optimize for that. We want to optimize for the one day, the people that are doing it on the spot. If however your product takes a bit more decision or you've got email sequences on the back to warm them up or anything like that, you may want to select seven days. You're giving Facebook more data to choose how it optimizes for your ads.

## [06:06 - 08:26]

The default is always going to be seven days and typically this is what Facebook recommends as well unless you actually know your audience and you know where your conversions are, how fast your conversions are coming through. This will typically be the default. If you go and switch this, so if you're testing out an ad set, it's okay to go and switch this to a different conversion window within an existing ad set that's running. However just be aware of a couple things here. The first thing is when you make a big change like this, you really have to give it at least a week, at least a week especially if you're going from one day to seven days because all of a sudden Facebook needs more data to base its decision off of. So give it at least a week before you make a decision whether or not it's working. In fact Facebook says a couple of weeks here, but it should be fine after a week. The second thing here is this is using a conversion campaign here and just like any conversion campaign, if you are trying to optimize for a specific conversion, you really need at least 15, minimum 15 to 20 of those conversion event triggers per ad set, per week. That is the recommended best practice way to properly optimize Facebook because Facebook needs data. It doesn't know the difference between a coincidental conversion or a cause conversion that your ads created. So it really needs that data for statistical purposes. So this means that if you are optimizing for purchases right at the top up here and essentially you, this doesn't have a pixel so it's not going to work, but you're optimizing for purchases at the top and you're only getting three purchases per week, well then you may want to switch that optimization to add to cart or view content or something that is getting you at least 15 to 20 event triggers per ad set per week. So that's the difference between conversion windows and attribution windows and essentially how to manage and leverage both of those inside of your campaigns.
